name: linux-shell-scripting
description: This skill should be used when the user asks to \"create bash scripts\", \"automate Linux tasks\", \"monitor system resources\", \"backup files\", \"manage users\", or \"write production she...
capabilities: Generate production-ready bash scripts for file and database backups with rotation
Create system monitoring scripts for CPU, disk, memory, and health checks
Build user management scripts for account creation and password expiry tracking
Develop security scripts for password generation and file encryption using OpenSSL
Write log analysis utilities for error extraction and web server traffic analysis
Automate system administration tasks including package installation, cron scheduling, and service management
author: zebbern
securitySummary: Static analysis detected 170 patterns in SKILL.md markdown documentation. All findings are false positives - they represent bash code examples within markdown code blocks, not executable code. The skill provides legitimate educational shell scripting templates for system administration tasks. No malicious intent or execution vectors found.
valueStatement: Manually writing bash scripts for system administration is time-consuming and error-prone. This skill provides production-ready shell script templates for common Linux tasks including backups, monitoring, user management, log analysis, and automation.
userTitle: Create Linux Shell Scripts for Automation
limitations: Scripts require Linux/Unix environment with bash shell and appropriate system permissions
Some scripts need additional utilities installed (rsync, openssl, mysql, curl, etc.)
Templates may require customization for specific distributions or use cases
Database backup scripts are MySQL-specific and need adaptation for other databases

useCases.title: Automated Backup Systems
useCases.description: System administrator needs scheduled backup scripts for databases and directories with automatic rotation to prevent disk space issues.

useCases.title: Server Monitoring Dashboards
useCases.description: DevOps engineer requires resource monitoring scripts that track CPU, disk, and memory usage with alerting capabilities.

useCases.title: Security Audit Automation
useCases.description: Security analyst needs scripts to automate user management, password expiry checks, and log analysis for compliance reporting.

promptTemplates.title: Create Basic Backup Script
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a bash script that backs up the /var/www directory to /backup with a timestamp. Keep only the last 7 backups and delete older ones automatically.

promptTemplates.title: Monitor Server Resources
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a monitoring script that checks CPU and disk usage every 5 minutes. Send an email alert if CPU exceeds 80% or disk usage goes above 90%. Log all checks to /var/log/monitor.log

promptTemplates.title: Encrypt Sensitive Files
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a script that encrypts all files in /data/sensitive using AES-256 encryption. The script should accept a password from stdin, encrypt files with .enc extension, and verify the encrypted files can be decrypted.

promptTemplates.title: Automated Log Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Build a comprehensive log analyzer for Apache/Nginx access logs. Extract top 20 IP addresses by request count, identify suspicious patterns (404 errors, SQL injection attempts), and generate daily HTML reports saved to /var/log/reports.
promptTemplates.scenario: Advanced log parsing and security analysis

bestPractices: Always test scripts in a non-production environment first to verify behavior and prevent data loss
Use absolute paths instead of relative paths in scripts to avoid execution context issues
Quote all variable references to handle filenames with spaces and special characters properly
Enable bash debugging mode with 'set -x' during development and 'set -e' to exit on errors
antiPatterns: Avoid using eval command or unquoted variable interpolation which can lead to code injection vulnerabilities
Never hardcode passwords or API keys in scripts - use environment variables or secure credential managers instead
Do not skip error checking - always verify command exit codes using $? or set -e for proper error handling
Avoid removing files with rm -rf without confirmation checks and explicit path validation

faq.question: Do these scripts work on all Linux distributions?
faq.answer: Most scripts work across distributions but some commands differ. For example, package management uses apt on Debian/Ubuntu but yum/dnf on RHEL/CentOS. You may need to adjust paths and commands for your distribution.

faq.question: Can I run these scripts on macOS?
faq.answer: Many scripts work on macOS as it uses bash, but some Linux-specific commands and paths differ. Test thoroughly and modify commands like 'ps', 'netstat', or file system paths (/proc doesn't exist on macOS).

faq.question: How do I schedule these scripts to run automatically?
faq.answer: Use cron for scheduling. Run 'crontab -e' and add entries using the format: minute hour day month weekday /path/to/script.sh. For example, '0 2 * * * /backup/nightly.sh' runs daily at 2 AM.

faq.question: What permissions do I need to run these scripts?
faq.answer: Basic scripts need execute permission (chmod +x script.sh). System administration tasks like user management or service control require root or sudo privileges. Always use the minimum privilege necessary.

faq.question: How can I debug a script that isn't working?
faq.answer: Run with bash -x script.sh to enable debug tracing that shows each command. Add 'set -e' at the top to exit on errors. Check log files, verify paths with 'which command', and test individual commands interactively.

faq.question: Are these scripts safe for production environments?
faq.answer: Templates are production-quality but require testing and customization for your specific environment. Always test in staging first, validate paths and thresholds, implement proper error handling, and set up monitoring before production deployment.
